name: Vietnamese Government/Institutional
summary: Red + gold on white, formal, mirrors official documents and flag

typography.vietnamese_diacritics: Excellent - Be Vietnam Pro is purpose-built for Vietnamese with adaptive diacritics for stacked marks like ượ, ễ, ắ

animation_style.approach: Restrained and formal - fade-ins, gentle slides, no bouncy springs
animation_style.entrance_pattern: Slide-up with fade, 400-600ms duration, ease-out curve
animation_style.motion_budget: Low - government/institutional style demands gravitas, not playfulness

projector_readability.contrast_approach: Dark text on white/cream backgrounds ensures maximum projector brightness utilization
projector_readability.min_font_sizes: Body 28px, headings 38px+, labels 32px - all well above legibility thresholds at distance
projector_readability.color_washout: Red (#C8102E) and gold (#FFCD00) are both high-saturation colors that resist projector washout well; gold especially vibrant on white
projector_readability.line_weight: Borders and dividers at 2-3px minimum to survive projection blur

wcag_contrast_score.red_on_white: #C8102E on #FFFFFF = 5.6:1 (AA pass, AAA fail for small text)
wcag_contrast_score.gold_on_white: #FFCD00 on #FFFFFF = 1.5:1 (FAIL - gold text on white is not readable, use only for decorative elements)
wcag_contrast_score.overall: AA compliant for all functional text; decorative gold reserved for borders/icons only

projector_degradation_resistance.details: Light background leverages projector's strongest capability (producing white light). Red and gold are saturated enough to remain distinct even with 30% contrast loss. White surface means blacks stay darkest element. Thin gold lines may blur but thick accents survive.

vietnamese_diacritics_rendering.font_suitability: Be Vietnam Pro is the gold standard for Vietnamese rendering - engineered specifically for Vietnamese diacritics with adaptive positioning
vietnamese_diacritics_rendering.line_height_needs: line-height 1.5-1.6 recommended to prevent stacked diacritics (e.g., ượ) from clipping into line above
vietnamese_diacritics_rendering.min_size_for_clarity: 28px minimum ensures diacritics are distinguishable even on classroom projectors at 5-10m viewing distance

cultural_fit.alignment: Perfect alignment with Vietnamese governmental and national identity
cultural_fit.details: Red (#C8102E) and gold (#FFCD00) are the exact flag colors of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam. The formal, institutional style mirrors official government documents, Communist Party communications, and national ceremony aesthetics. For a topic on 'national unity' (đại đoàn kết toàn dân tộc), this palette directly invokes state symbolism.
cultural_fit.risk: May feel too politically heavy-handed or propagandistic for an academic presentation; could be perceived as unoriginal student effort

cultural_semiotics.red: Revolution, bloodshed of independence struggle, Communist Party, national sovereignty
cultural_semiotics.gold_star: Five classes of Vietnamese society united (workers, peasants, soldiers, intellectuals, businessmen), also prosperity
cultural_semiotics.white_background: Official document authority, clarity, institutional legitimacy
cultural_semiotics.overall: Strongly invokes state/party identity - powerful for national unity topic but may overplay political symbolism

teacher_perception_signal.details: Teacher sees students who respect the subject matter and align with official framing. Low creative risk but also low impression of design sophistication. May look like every other student presentation that defaults to flag colors.
teacher_perception_signal.effort_perception: Moderate - obvious color choice doesn't demonstrate design thinking

rubric_alignment.format_15pct: Functional but generic - red/gold/white is clean and readable but doesn't showcase creative formatting effort
rubric_alignment.theory_35pct: Strong support - formal institutional framing naturally suits academic theory presentation with authority
rubric_alignment.application_50pct: Adequate - can display data and real-world examples but the formality may make application content feel dry rather than engaging

content_density_capacity.data_viz_support: Good - clean white backgrounds are ideal canvas for charts/graphs; red accent works for bar charts and highlights
content_density_capacity.theory_density: High - formal style naturally accommodates dense text blocks with clear hierarchy

production_effort.migration_cost: Low-medium - current codebase already uses red/gold palette (B91C1C/D97706). Shifting to official flag colors (C8102E/FFCD00) and making style more formal requires palette swap and removing playful animation curves
production_effort.component_changes: Minimal - existing DS components (GlassPanel, SectionTitle, etc.) work with palette swap; may need to add formal borders and government-style header bars

remotion_implementation_complexity.css_needs: Simple - solid colors, sharp borders, minimal gradients
remotion_implementation_complexity.threejs_usage: Can reduce/remove 3D background - formal style doesn't need it
remotion_implementation_complexity.render_performance: Fast - no complex shaders or heavy animations

distraction_level: Low - design is utilitarian and lets content lead, but also visually unremarkable
differentiation: Low - this is the default 'safe' choice for Vietnamese political topics; likely what 50% of student groups will produce

temporal_coherence.consistency: High - red/gold/white is a tight palette that naturally stays coherent across 13.5 minutes
temporal_coherence.section_transitions: Easy to maintain with consistent header bars and section dividers
temporal_coherence.speaker_differentiation: Can use member accent colors within the red-gold spectrum but palette is narrow

dark_vs_light_mode_viability: Locked to light mode (white/cream backgrounds). Dark mode would undermine the institutional feel and projector readability. This is correct for the use case.

examples: Vietnamese government official websites (chinhphu.vn) - red headers, gold accents on white
Communist Party of Vietnam publications and posters
Vietnamese national ceremony stage backdrops and banners
precedent.notes: This style has strong real-world precedent in Vietnamese institutional contexts but is rarely seen as a deliberate design choice in student presentations - usually it's a default rather than an intentional aesthetic
